description: Animas Forks was a silver mining camp perched at 11,200 feet in the San Juan Mountains of San Juan County, Colorado, booming in the 1870s-1880s when hundreds of miners worked the surrounding claims despite brutal winters that buried the buildings to their rooftops. The town had a post office, general store, saloon, and several boarding houses before declining silver prices and avalanche danger drove the last residents out by the early 1900s. Several remarkably preserved structures still stand today, including the iconic Bay Window House, accessible via a rugged 4WD road from Silverton. The high-altitude townsite and surrounding mine dumps are rich territory for finding period artifacts, mining tokens, and hand-forged tools.

name: Aldridge, MT
description: Aldridge was a small Montana mining camp in Park County in the Paradise Valley at the northern edge of Yellowstone country, established in the 1890s to support coal mining operations that supplied fuel to the Northern Pacific Railroad's smelting and processing operations in the region. The coal mines operated for roughly two decades before exhaustion of the economically viable seams forced closure, and the camp was abandoned in the early twentieth century. The site today is on private land in the northern Yellowstone gateway area, where ranching and tourism have supplanted the old mining economy. Former coal mining camps in Park County can yield early twentieth century mining equipment and domestic artifacts from the company housing era.

name: Bonanza, ID
description: Bonanza was a silver mining camp in Custer County, Idaho in the heart of the Salmon River Mountains, established in the 1870s after rich silver and lead deposits were discovered in the upper Yankee Fork of the Salmon River. The town boomed through the 1870s-1880s with saloons, stores, and a small hotel serving the mining population before the ore ran out and the population dispersed. A small number of historic structures survive in the valley, accessible via the Yankee Fork Road through the Challis National Forest. The Yankee Fork Dredge, a massive gold dredge that worked the river valley downstream, is now preserved as a state historic site nearby.

name: Pleasant View, WA
description: Pleasant View was a small agricultural community in Stevens County, Washington in the upper Columbia River basin region of northeastern Washington state, established by homesteaders in the late nineteenth century who farmed the rolling hills and river bottoms of this fertile agricultural territory. The community served the surrounding farms with a post office and local commerce before the regional economy consolidated in larger centers like Colville and Chewelah. The site is now private farmland in Stevens County, whose agricultural landscape has remained remarkably consistent with its nineteenth-century character. Detecting in former Stevens County farming communities with landowner permission can yield homestead-era agricultural and domestic artifacts.

name: Poland, AZ
description: Poland was a mining camp in Yavapai County, Arizona in the Bradshaw Mountains south of Prescott, established in the 1870s near gold and silver mining claims in the rugged central Arizona mountain terrain. The camp developed around the Congress and nearby mines but never grew into a substantial town, existing primarily as a supply point for the surrounding prospectors and miners. The site today is in the Prescott National Forest in the Bradshaw Mountains, with old mine workings and camp remnants scattered across the rugged terrain. The national forest land in this area generally requires a permit for artifact recovery, though the surrounding prospecting history makes the Bradshaw Mountains a popular destination for rockhounders and history enthusiasts.

name: Bodie, CA
description: Bodie is California's most famous gold mining ghost town, perched at 8,375 feet in the eastern Sierra Nevada in Mono County, booming from the late 1870s through the 1880s when the Standard Mine produced millions in gold and a rough population of several thousand miners, gamblers, and opportunists created one of the West's most notoriously wild frontier towns. Bodie's reputation for lawlessness, with gunfights and robberies reported almost daily at its peak, made it a byword for frontier violence — the local newspaper reported 'a man for breakfast every morning.' The town survived in arrested decline through the twentieth century, and in 1962 it became Bodie State Historic Park, now preserved in a state of 'arrested decay' — frozen as it was abandoned. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited within the state park, but the site itself — with over 100 original structures remaining — is one of America's most spectacular historic preservation sites.

name: Reveille, NV
description: Reveille was a small silver mining camp in Nye County, Nevada in the remote Reveille Range of the central Nevada Basin and Range country, established in the 1860s when silver strikes drew prospectors to these isolated mountains in one of America's most sparsely populated regions. The camp had a brief but productive mining period before the accessible ore was exhausted and the miners moved on to fresher strikes, leaving the desert to reclaim the townsite. The remote central Nevada location — accessible only via long stretches of unpaved desert road — means the site sees relatively few visitors today. The surrounding BLM desert land offers open access, and the high-desert climate of central Nevada preserves metallic artifacts exceptionally well.

name: Lime City, IA
description: Lime City was a small Iowa community in Hamilton County that developed in the mid-nineteenth century around limestone quarrying operations along the Des Moines River, supplying building stone and lime for construction throughout central Iowa during the great period of prairie settlement. The community faded as concrete and alternative building materials supplanted lime in construction and the local quarry operations declined. The site today is private farmland along the Des Moines River valley in Hamilton County. Central Iowa river valley communities from the limestone quarrying era can yield interesting industrial artifacts alongside standard homestead-era domestic items.

name: Lorraine, PA
description: Lorraine was a small agricultural community in Butler County, Pennsylvania in the rolling farmland of western Pennsylvania, briefly existing as a post office and local service point for surrounding farm families before being absorbed into the regional agricultural landscape. Western Pennsylvania communities from the mid-to-late nineteenth century carry a rich history tied to the canal era, early oil industry, and agricultural settlement, with Butler County positioned at the heart of Pennsylvania's early oil country. Former rural communities in Butler County can yield artifacts from the county's agricultural and early oil industry heritage.

name: Shakespeare, NM
description: Shakespeare was a fading New Mexico ghost town in Hidalgo County near Lordsburg in the bootheel region of the state, originally established as a silver and copper mining camp called Ralston City in 1870 and later renamed Shakespeare when subsequent ore discoveries reinvigorated the camp in the 1880s. The town hosted colorful characters including Billy the Kid and Russian Bill Tettenborn, the latter of whom was hanged in the town's dining room by vigilantes in 1881. Shakespeare is now privately owned and operated as a living history site by the Graham family, who conduct periodic tours of the remarkably intact adobe structures that make this one of New Mexico's best-preserved territorial-era mining towns. The site is only accessible during guided tours, and artifact collecting on private property requires explicit landowner permission.

name: Zzyzx, CA
description: Zzyzx was a mineral springs resort in San Bernardino County, California in the Mojave Desert along the old Salt Lake Road, developed in the 1940s by the flamboyant radio evangelist Curtis Howe Springer who operated the 'Zzyzx Mineral Springs and Health Resort' on fraudulently claimed federal land for over three decades, marketing his radio programs and dubious health cures to a Depression and war-weary audience. Springer was evicted from the federal land in 1974, and the site was subsequently converted into the Desert Studies Center, an educational consortium of California State University campuses. The name Zzyzx — invented by Springer to create 'the last word in the dictionary' — remains the official USGS designation. The Desert Studies Center operates year-round on the site, and the mineral spring pool and original Spanish Mission-style buildings are a fascinating relic of Springer's eccentric empire.

name: Fayette, MI
description: Fayette was one of Michigan's finest industrial ghost towns, a complete iron smelting community on a protected harbor along the Garden Peninsula of Delta County in the Upper Peninsula, built and operated by the Jackson Iron Company from 1867 to 1891 to process Upper Peninsula iron ore into pig iron using locally produced charcoal. At its peak, Fayette had a hotel, opera house, doctor's office, and over 20 production buildings housing over 500 workers and their families in company-owned housing. The smelting operation closed in 1891 when natural gas and coke-fueled furnaces elsewhere became more economical, and the company sold its holdings. Today Fayette Historic Townsite within Fayette Township is preserved as a state park, with over 20 original structures standing — one of Michigan's most important industrial heritage sites.

name: Rawsonville, MI
description: Rawsonville was a small Michigan community in Washtenaw County that existed as a mill and agricultural center along the Huron River in the nineteenth century, briefly supporting a post office and commercial activity before being overshadowed by larger Washtenaw County communities. The Huron River corridor in Washtenaw County has been continuously settled since the early 1820s and is now largely suburban territory within the Ann Arbor metropolitan area. Former mill sites along the Huron River in Washtenaw County occasionally yield mill hardware, millstones, and nineteenth-century industrial artifacts.

name: Central, MI
description: Central was a small copper mining camp in Keweenaw County, Michigan on the Keweenaw Peninsula in the Lake Superior copper district, established in the 1850s as part of the great copper rush that made Keweenaw County one of America's most productive copper mining regions for the latter half of the nineteenth century. The community housed miners and their families in company housing around the Central Mine shaft, which produced rich native copper deposits until operations ceased in the 1890s when easily accessible ore was exhausted. The Central Mine site includes a restored Methodist church that has been the focus of periodic heritage services by descendants of the Cornish and other immigrant miners who worked the Keweenaw copper district. The Keweenaw Peninsula landscape today is a National Heritage Area, with many historic mining sites open to exploration.

name: Pere Cheney, MI
description: Pere Cheney was a small lumbering community in Crawford County, Michigan in the northern Lower Peninsula that boomed briefly in the 1870s-1880s when the surrounding white pine forests were being clearcut at an industrial pace, reaching a population of several hundred before the timber was exhausted and the lumber companies moved their operations northward. The town's remaining residents — already battered by smallpox and typhoid epidemics — were finally driven off by the complete exhaustion of the surrounding forests, and Pere Cheney was abandoned by the early 1890s. The site today features a haunted reputation and an accessible old cemetery surrounded by second-growth jack pine and aspen, on land that is now state forest. The cemetery and surrounding area are accessible, and the site is one of northern Michigan's most frequently visited ghost towns.

name: Glen Haven, MI
description: Glen Haven was a small Great Lakes maritime community on the Sleeping Bear Dunes peninsula in Leelanau County, Michigan that existed from the 1850s as a steamship wooding station where Lake Michigan vessels stopped to take on cordwood fuel, later transitioning to fruit farming as the maritime economy changed. The community had a hotel, general store, and a Life-Saving Service station that served the dangerous Manitou Passage between the mainland and the Manitou Islands. The entire Glen Haven area is now within Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore, established in 1970, which has preserved several original buildings including the old Coast Guard boathouse and general store. The national lakeshore designation prohibits metal detecting, but the remarkably preserved maritime village is one of the Midwest's finest examples of an intact Victorian-era Lake Michigan port community.

name: Old Victoria, MI
description: Old Victoria was a copper and silver mining community in Ontonagon County, Michigan on the Keweenaw Peninsula's southern reaches, established in the 1840s during the early years of the great Lake Superior copper rush as miners explored the volcanic trap rock formations of the Lake Superior Copper District. The Victoria Mine produced modest quantities of copper through the mid-nineteenth century before being largely abandoned, and the community that grew around it faded with the mine. Several restored log buildings from the mining era have been preserved at the Victoria Dam site by Upper Peninsula Power Company, creating a small outdoor museum. The Ontonagon County landscape retains strong links to its copper mining heritage, and old mine dumps and settlement sites in the region can be productive detecting territory on accessible public and private lands.

name: Deward, MI
description: Deward was a quintessential Michigan lumber ghost town in Kalkaska County that was built entirely by the D.H. Day lumber company in 1901, grew to over 1,000 residents at its peak with a complete company town infrastructure, and was entirely demolished and abandoned by 1912 when the surrounding white and Norway pine forest was completely logged out. Day's meticulous records of the town's construction and demolition make Deward unusual among Michigan lumber ghost towns in being extremely well-documented historically. The site today is state forest land in Kalkaska County, accessible and occasionally visited by history enthusiasts and metal detectorists. Old logging camp and company town sites in Michigan's northern Lower Peninsula can yield impressive collections of period tools, coins, and personal items.

name: Gibbs City, MI
description: Gibbs City was another Upper Peninsula Michigan copper mining community in Iron County, established during the period of intensive mining exploration in the Lake Superior copper region in the second half of the nineteenth century. The mining operation at Gibbs City never achieved the scale of the major Keweenaw copper producers and the community faded relatively quickly when copper production proved economically marginal at this location. The Upper Peninsula of Michigan is dotted with the ruins of such small copper and iron mining operations, most now on state or national forest land. Old mining camp sites in this region can yield period mining equipment, company scrip, and immigrant-era personal items.

name: Jacktown, MI
description: Jacktown was a small Michigan Upper Peninsula community in Dickinson County near the Menominee Iron Range that developed as a support settlement for the iron mining operations of northern Michigan, existing through the latter decades of the nineteenth century before the consolidation of mining operations into larger, more efficient facilities bypassed smaller communities like Jacktown. Iron County and Dickinson County in the Upper Peninsula have an extensive iron mining heritage, with numerous small communities having risen and fallen alongside the fortunes of individual mines and ore ranges. Former iron range communities in this part of Michigan are now largely on state or national forest land, with some sites accessible for historical exploration.

name: Nonesuch, MI
description: Nonesuch was a copper mining camp in Ontonagon County, Michigan in the Lake Superior Copper District, named with the self-deprecating or perhaps hopeful humor common to mining camp nomenclature, established in the 1860s-1870s during the great period of copper exploration across the Keweenaw Peninsula and surrounding counties. The mine produced modest quantities of copper before operations proved economically marginal, and the camp was abandoned. The remote Ontonagon County landscape where Nonesuch sat is now largely state or national forest land, preserving the wild character of the Lake Superior copper country. Old mine dumps and camp foundations from the Copper Country era are scattered across much of this region and can be explored on accessible public lands.

name: Singapore, MI
description: Singapore was a thriving Michigan lumber town in Allegan County at the mouth of the Kalamazoo River on Lake Michigan, established in the 1830s as a speculative real estate venture and growing into a significant lumber shipping port before being completely buried by migrating sand dunes in the 1870s. At its peak Singapore had three hotels, two sawmills, a bank, and a general store, serving as a significant Lake Michigan port before the combination of the Chicago Fire of 1871 (which consumed enormous quantities of Michigan lumber) and the advancing dunes overwhelmed the community. The town was gradually buried by sand dunes typical of the Lake Michigan shoreline, and today Singapore lies beneath the dunes at the mouth of the Kalamazoo River near Saugatuck. Parts of the buried town occasionally surface during major storms that shift the dune landscape.

name: Trow, WI
description: Trow was a small Wisconsin farming community in Sauk County in the Baraboo Hills region, a brief post-office community that developed during the great Wisconsin agricultural settlement era of the mid-to-late nineteenth century and faded as the regional economy consolidated in larger market towns. The Sauk County landscape is notable for its dramatic geology — ancient quartzite ridges, Parfrey's Glen, and Devils Lake — and the surrounding countryside has been farmed continuously since the 1840s. Former rural Sauk County communities like Trow are now private farmland, but the region's long agricultural history makes it productive territory for detecting with landowner permission.

name: Tecoma, NV
description: Tecoma was a small Nevada mining community in Elko County near the Utah border in the Toano Range, established in the 1870s when silver discoveries drew prospectors to this remote corner of the Basin and Range country in northeastern Nevada. The community served as a supply point for surrounding mining operations and had a brief commercial existence before declining ore values and the marginal nature of the deposits led to abandonment. The remote northeastern Nevada landscape is primarily BLM land and cattle ranching country today, with old mining camp sites accessible along the network of desert roads. The dry Great Basin climate preserves metallic artifacts well, making former mining camp sites in this region productive for detecting on BLM land.

name: Monroe City, TX
description: Monroe City was a small agricultural community in Angelina County, Texas in the East Texas Piney Woods region, one of the many small farming and timber communities that developed in the post-Civil War era as East Texas was settled by families from the Deep South establishing cotton farms and sawmill operations in the longleaf pine forest. The community faded as the timber industry depleted the forest and railroads consolidated commercial activity in larger East Texas towns. The site today is in the privately held farmland and second-growth pine forest typical of Angelina County's rural landscape. East Texas former plantation and timber communities can yield interesting Civil War and Reconstruction-era artifacts with landowner permission.

name: Arlington, MO
description: Arlington was a small agricultural community in Pettis County, Missouri in the rolling farmland of west-central Missouri that developed in the mid-nineteenth century as a local service point for surrounding farms but failed to grow beyond a modest post office community when railroad lines bypassed the site. Pettis County, home to Sedalia, was an important livestock shipping point and agricultural center in nineteenth-century Missouri, and many small communities in the county existed primarily to support the cattle drives and farm trade. The site today is private agricultural land in the Missouri River drainage region. Former Missouri post office communities typically yield mid-nineteenth century coinage and domestic artifacts when detecting with landowner permission.

name: Glenrio, TX/NM
description: Glenrio straddles the Texas-New Mexico border in Quay County, NM and Deaf Smith County, TX along historic US Route 66, originally established as a Texas and Pacific Railway water stop in 1903 and later growing into a classic Route 66 tourist service community with gas stations, cafes, and motels serving cross-country travelers during the highway's golden age from the 1940s through the 1960s. The state line between Texas and New Mexico runs through the middle of town, creating an unusual situation where the businesses on the Texas side sold beer (legal in that county) and New Mexico side services took advantage of different tax laws. Interstate 40 bypassed Glenrio in 1973, immediately killing the remaining businesses, and today the town is a Route 66 ghost town with several original buildings still standing in the windswept high plains landscape. The site is partly accessible from the old highway alignment, and abandoned Route 66 roadside communities yield period gas station equipment, motel hardware, and automotive-era artifacts.

name: Newkirk, NM
description: Newkirk was a small Guadalupe County, New Mexico community along historic US Route 66 in the eastern New Mexico high plains, one of several tiny service communities that existed along the highway corridor to provide gas, food, and water to travelers crossing the vast Llano Estacado and Canadian River breaks landscape. Like many Route 66 roadside communities in this stretch of New Mexico, Newkirk died quickly when Interstate 40 diverted the cross-country traffic away from the old highway. A few remaining structures mark the original townsite along the ghost highway alignment. The surrounding eastern New Mexico landscape is working ranch country with private land requiring permission for any detecting activity.

name: Spencer, MO
description: Spencer was a small Ralls County, Missouri agricultural community along the North Salt River in the northeastern Missouri uplands, briefly platted and supporting a post office in the nineteenth century before being absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape as the county's commercial activity concentrated in railroad towns like New London and Hannibal. Northeast Missouri's rural communities carry a rich ante-bellum and Civil War history, as this region was bitterly contested along the Missouri-Kansas border during the 1860s. Former Missouri community sites in this region can yield interesting period artifacts from both the agricultural and Civil War eras.

name: Amboy, CA
description: Amboy was a Mojave Desert community in San Bernardino County, California along historic Route 66, built around Roy's Motel and Cafe — opened in 1938 — which became one of the most photographed stopping points on the entire Route 66 highway corridor due to its distinctive 1950s sign and remote desert setting amid the Mojave volcanic landscape near Amboy Crater. The community served cross-country travelers, military personnel from nearby bases, and adventurers through the Route 66 era before Interstate 40 bypassed the site and the population dwindled to near zero. Roy's has been periodically restored and operates intermittently as a tourist attraction today, drawing Route 66 enthusiasts and photographers to the Mojave Desert landmark. The surrounding BLM land includes the Amboy Crater National Natural Landmark, a perfectly preserved cinder cone volcano accessible by a short trail.

name: Anaconda, NM
description: Anaconda was a small copper and silver mining camp in Grant County, New Mexico in the Black Range mountains of southwestern New Mexico, established in the mining boom of the 1880s when prospectors spread across the rugged Mogollon country seeking base and precious metals. The remote mountain location and modest ore deposits limited Anaconda's development, and the camp was abandoned relatively quickly as the more productive mines elsewhere in Grant County captured investment. The surrounding Gila National Forest covers much of Grant County's mountain landscape, making many former mining sites accessible on national forest land. The Gila National Forest generally requires a permit for significant artifact collection, though surface observation is typically permitted.

name: Bagdad, CA
description: Bagdad was a San Bernardino County, California community along the Santa Fe Railway in the Mojave Desert, one of the classic Route 66 ghost towns that served as a railroad water stop and small commercial community until the combination of railroad dieselization, Route 66 bypass by Interstate 40, and the brutal Mojave Desert climate drained the life from the community over several decades. The town held a post office and cafe for decades, serving travelers and railroad workers in the searing heat of the Mojave. Today only scattered foundations and the old railroad right-of-way mark the site, which is surrounded by the vast BLM-managed Mojave Desert landscape. The accessible desert terrain makes this area explorable, and old railroad-era sites in the Mojave can yield interesting period artifacts.

name: Boise, TX
description: Boise City (Boise, TX) in Montague County was a small North Texas farming community distinct from the better-known Boise City in Oklahoma, existing as a modest agricultural service point in the Texas blackland prairie region in the late nineteenth century before larger neighboring communities captured the regional trade. The North Texas landscape around Montague County has been continuously farmed since the 1860s-1870s settlement era and retains its agricultural character today. Former Texas farming communities in this region of the state can yield interesting frontier-era artifacts including Spanish colonial-era items in some locations.

name: Chambless, CA
description: Chambless was a Mojave Desert community in San Bernardino County, California along Route 66 between Amboy and Ludlow, established as a railroad water pumping station and later developing into a small roadside community serving Route 66 travelers with gas and basic services in the early automobile era. The town's water pump served the Santa Fe Railway's steam locomotives crossing the Mojave before dieselization made the stops unnecessary, eliminating the town's primary reason for existing. Interstate 40 dealt the final blow, and today only old concrete foundations and the stripped bones of former buildings remain in the creosote flats. The surrounding Mojave Desert BLM land is open for exploration, and railroad-era and automotive-era artifacts from Route 66 communities in this stretch of the desert are of interest to collectors.

name: Clementine, MO
description: Clementine was a small Gasconade County, Missouri farming community along the Gasconade River in the Ozark foothills of central Missouri, briefly supporting a post office and local commercial activity during the post-Civil War settlement era of the Missouri Ozarks. The rugged Gasconade River country attracted German immigrant farmers in the nineteenth century, and many small communities like Clementine developed to serve these agricultural settlements before consolidating in larger centers. The Gasconade River valley today is a popular outdoor recreation corridor in the Missouri Ozarks, with river access and state conservation land interspersed with private farm and woodland property.

name: Essex, CA
description: Essex was a San Bernardino County, California Route 66 community in the eastern Mojave Desert, established as a railroad siding and small community along the Santa Fe mainline between Needles and Barstow, serving both railroad workers and highway travelers who needed gas, food, and water before or after the long desert crossing. The Providence Mountains State Recreation Area is nearby, featuring Mitchell Caverns — a remarkable cave system that once drew tourists to the eastern Mojave before the facility was temporarily closed by budget cuts. Essex survives today as a tiny community with a gas station and cafe still operating, making it one of the few surviving service points on this stretch of the old Route 66 alignment. The surrounding desert BLM land includes old mine sites in the Providence Mountains that are of interest to prospectors and history enthusiasts.

name: Hackberry, AZ
description: Hackberry was an Arizona Route 66 community in Mohave County along the old National Old Trails Road corridor through the western Arizona desert, originally established as a mining camp near silver and lead deposits in the Hackberry area before transitioning to a Route 66 service community in the highway era. The town had a gas station, general store, and basic services for travelers crossing the Mojave Desert portion of Route 66 between Kingman and Seligman. The Hackberry General Store has been preserved and restored as a Route 66 landmark and museum, drawing history enthusiasts and road trippers to this classic Arizona desert community. The surrounding Mohave County landscape is BLM territory with old mine workings from the Hackberry Mining District accessible to prospectors and history enthusiasts.

name: Hext, OK
description: Hext was a small Beckham County, Oklahoma agricultural community in the western Oklahoma red clay plains region, one of the many modest farming communities that developed in this former Indian Territory after the land runs opened the area to settlement in the 1890s-1900s. The western Oklahoma farming economy, already marginal in normal years, was devastated by the Dust Bowl of the 1930s, which drove population from the region at catastrophic rates and collapsed many small communities that never recovered. The site today is private farmland in Beckham County's working agricultural landscape. Former Dust Bowl-era Oklahoma communities can yield interesting 1930s-era domestic artifacts alongside earlier homestead items from the land run era.

name: Holman, MO
description: Holman was a small Gasconade County, Missouri community in the Missouri Ozark hill country that developed as a farming and timbering support community during the late nineteenth century in the cedar and hardwood uplands of central Missouri. The Ozark hill country communities of Gasconade County were settled largely by German immigrants from the 1840s-1860s, creating a distinctive cultural landscape that persists in the region's architecture, church denominations, and agricultural traditions. Former Holman area sites in this region reflect both the German immigrant heritage and the broader Ozark frontier experience from the Civil War era onward.

name: Hooker, MO
description: Plano was a small Missouri community in Phelps County in the eastern Ozarks along the Gasconade River, briefly supporting a post office and modest commerce during the mid-nineteenth century before the regional economy consolidated. The Gasconade River valley in Phelps County is noted for its rich Civil War history, as guerrilla warfare between Unionist and Confederate sympathizers made this region extremely violent during the 1860s. The Ozark landscape retains its wild character, with much of Phelps County still forested and sparsely settled. Old community sites along Missouri's Gasconade River corridor can yield pre- and Civil War-era artifacts.

name: Plano, MO
description: Hooker was a small Texas County, Missouri community in the Ozark uplands of southern Missouri, a modest farming and timber support community that existed briefly in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries in this sparsely settled Ozark region. Texas County in the Missouri Ozarks contains some of Missouri's wildest and most remote landscape, with the Jacks Fork and Current rivers cutting through spectacular dolomite bluffs in what is now the Ozark National Scenic Riverways. Former Ozark hill country communities in this region are now largely private and national forest land, and the Civil War guerrilla history of Texas County adds historical significance to the area.

name: Siberia, CA
description: Siberia was a Mojave Desert community in San Bernardino County, California along Route 66 between Barstow and Needles, existing as a railroad water stop along the Santa Fe Railway in the early twentieth century before Route 66 brought automobile travelers through this stretch of the empty Mojave. Like most of the tiny service communities along this section of Route 66, Siberia was completely bypassed by Interstate 40 and then slowly consumed by the desert. The name — evocative of brutal cold in contrast to the scorching Mojave heat — was either chosen for its irony or as a reference to the isolation of the location. The surrounding Mojave Desert BLM land is open for exploration, and former railroad-era sites in the central Mojave yield interesting early twentieth century industrial artifacts.

name: Times Beach, MO
description: Times Beach was a small Missouri resort community in St. Louis County along the Meramec River established in the 1920s, initially developed as a vacation bungalow community where families could rent lots for summer recreation along the scenic Meramec River corridor outside St. Louis. The community transitioned from resort to year-round residence as working-class families moved in permanently, but became nationally infamous in 1983 when dioxin contamination from waste oil sprayed on dirt roads in the 1970s was found to be so severe that the EPA ordered the complete evacuation and purchase of the entire town. The structures were demolished, the contaminated soil was incinerated on-site, and the land was remediated before being converted to Route 66 State Park — one of America's most dramatic examples of an entire community erased by toxic contamination. The park today offers a remarkable juxtaposition of Meramec River recreation and the toxic legacy of industrial waste.

name: Two Guns, AZ
description: Two Guns was an Arizona roadside attraction and would-be town in Navajo County along historic Route 66, established in the 1920s by the flamboyant Harry 'Two Gun' Miller who built a dubious tourist attraction around the ruins of a Navajo Canyon trading post and nearby cliff dwellings, advertising it with homemade signs promoting a 'Death Cave' where Apache raiders were allegedly burned alive. The roadside enterprise changed hands several times, featured a fake zoo with mountain lions, and was the scene of multiple murders in its early years before declining as Route 66 traffic patterns changed. The ruins of Two Guns — including the caged animal enclosures, a stone trading post, and the Diablo Canyon bridge — are accessible from the old Route 66 alignment and remain one of the most eerie and atmospheric stops on the route. Metal detecting at the accessible ruins of this Route 66 attraction yields mid-twentieth century coins and automotive-era items.

name: Toronto, IL
description: Toronto was a small Woodford County, Illinois farming community that briefly held a post office in the mid-to-late nineteenth century while serving the surrounding agricultural operations in central Illinois's corn belt. The flat Woodford County prairie landscape was intensively settled and farmed beginning in the 1850s, and many small communities like Toronto that lacked railroad connections quickly gave way to better-positioned towns. The site today is private cropland in Woodford County, one of Illinois's most productive agricultural counties. Former Woodford County post office communities from the late nineteenth century can yield period agricultural and domestic artifacts with landowner permission.

name: Warm Springs, NV
description: Warm Springs was a Churchill County, Nevada community that existed as a watering hole and stage stop in the Great Basin desert along travel routes crossing the Basin and Range landscape of central Nevada, providing crucial water for livestock and travelers crossing the arid terrain between the Humboldt River and Carson Sink regions. The springs made Warm Springs an important stopping point in the pre-railroad era when overland travel across Nevada was a grueling multi-day ordeal. The site today is in the remote Churchill County desert, accessible via back roads through the vast BLM-managed Nevada landscape. Former Nevada stage stop communities in this region can yield overland era artifacts including harness hardware, coins, and personal items.

name: Ojibway, MI
description: Ojibway was a small Upper Peninsula Michigan community in Keweenaw County on the tip of the Keweenaw Peninsula, existing as a modest fishing and copper industry support settlement in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries in this remote Lake Superior shoreline area. The Keweenaw Peninsula, accessible by road only through Houghton County, is a remarkably preserved industrial heritage landscape, and Ojibway existed in the shadow of the larger copper mining operations that defined the region's economy. The Keweenaw National Historical Park encompasses much of the Keweenaw Peninsula's industrial heritage, and many historic sites are protected within the park's boundaries. The park's interpretive programs provide extensive historical context for this remarkable Michigan copper country landscape.

name: Phoenix, MI
description: Phoenix was a small copper mining community in Keweenaw County, Michigan on the Keweenaw Peninsula near Lac La Belle in the Lake Superior copper district, established during the great copper rush of the 1840s-1880s as part of the intensive mining development that made this remote Michigan peninsula one of America's most important industrial centers. The Phoenix mine was one of several in the lake district of the eastern Keweenaw that produced native copper from volcanic trap rock deposits unique to the Lake Superior basin. The community faded as the Phoenix Mine's ore production declined in the late nineteenth century. The surrounding Keweenaw Peninsula landscape today is a mix of national historical park, state and county land, and private property with variable access.

name: Delaware, MI
description: Delaware was a small Keweenaw County, Michigan copper mining community near Copper Harbor at the very tip of the Keweenaw Peninsula, established in the copper rush era and connected to the Delaware Mine, which produced copper from one of the peninsula's deeper mine shafts and operated through the 1880s before closing. The Delaware Mine shaft has been preserved as a tourist attraction, with guided underground tours available seasonally — one of the best opportunities to experience the underground world of the Lake Superior copper mining era. The community that supported the mine is largely gone, but the mine shaft, engine house ruins, and surrounding copper country landscape are preserved. The Keweenaw National Historical Park context makes this a historically rich but well-interpreted site.

name: Mandan, MI
description: Mandan was a small Upper Peninsula Michigan community in Houghton County at the heart of the Keweenaw copper mining district, connected to the Mandan Mine and the broader network of copper mining operations that made Houghton County one of the great industrial centers of nineteenth-century America. The community housed miners and their families in the close-knit residential patterns typical of Keweenaw copper mining communities, where Cornish, Finnish, Croatian, and other immigrant groups created distinct ethnic neighborhoods around the mine shafts. The Keweenaw Peninsula copper country is now recognized as a National Heritage Area, preserving the remarkable industrial and ethnic heritage of this remote Michigan peninsula.

name: Floresta, CO
description: Floresta was a small coal mining community in Huerfano County, Colorado on the eastern slope of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains near Walsenburg, established in the late nineteenth century to house miners working the rich coal seams of the Trinidad coalfield that powered southern Colorado's industrial development. The Colorado coalfields were the scene of intense labor strife in the early twentieth century, culminating in the 1913-1914 Coal Field War and the Ludlow Massacre (at a nearby site), which reshaped American labor law and the national conversation about worker rights. Floresta and its neighboring coal camps declined as coal production consolidated into larger operations and eventually diesel replaced coal in railroad operations. The Huerfano County landscape today retains old coal mine infrastructure visible from public roads.

name: Woodstock, CO
description: Woodstock was a small Huerfano County, Colorado coal mining community in the Cucharas River valley on the eastern slope of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, one of several coal camp communities that served the southern Colorado coalfields in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Colorado coal country communities were characterized by intense ethnicity — Italian, Slavic, Hispanic, and other immigrant groups filling the ranks of the mines — and fierce labor-management conflict that marked the region's history. Coal Camp life in Huerfano County was managed by company control of housing, stores, and political life until the labor reforms that followed the Ludlow Massacre of 1914. The remaining buildings and infrastructure of Woodstock and similar Huerfano County coal camps are largely on private mining land.

name: Pomme De Terre, MN
description: Pomme De Terre was a small Kandiyohi County, Minnesota farming community in the west-central Minnesota prairie that developed as an agricultural service point during the great homesteading era of the 1870s-1880s, briefly supporting a post office and local commerce for surrounding Scandinavian and German immigrant farm families. The community faded as the county's commercial activity consolidated in larger centers like Willmar, which became Kandiyohi County's dominant railroad town. The site today is private farmland in the productive west-central Minnesota agricultural landscape. Former Minnesota homestead communities in this region can yield Scandinavian immigrant-era household items and late Victorian coinage with landowner permission.

name: Cahaba, AL
description: Cahaba was Alabama's first state capital, established at the confluence of the Cahaba and Alabama rivers in Dallas County in 1820 and serving as the state seat of government until 1826 when flooding forced the capital to be relocated to Tuscaloosa. The town survived as a river port and market center through the antebellum era, becoming a significant cotton shipping point before the Civil War, but was severely damaged by floods and the war's aftermath and never fully recovered. Today the site is preserved as the Cahaba Archaeological Site and is managed by the Alabama Historical Commission, with regular public access. The rich history of this former state capital — including its colonial Spanish, Creek Indian, and antebellum layers — makes Cahaba one of Alabama's most significant archaeological destinations.

name: Kennicott, AK
description: Kennicott was a National Historic Landmark copper mining town in Wrangell-St. Elias National Park in Alaska, built by the Kennecott Copper Corporation starting in 1903 and operating until 1938, producing over $200 million in copper from the incredibly rich Bonanza and Jumbo ore bodies during its 35 years of operation. The remote Wrangell Mountains location required building the Copper River and Northwestern Railway — one of the most difficult railroad construction projects in American history — just to get the ore to tidewater at Cordova. When the ore ran out in 1938, the entire operation was shut down virtually overnight, and Kennicott and the nearby worker town of McCarthy were abandoned with much of the equipment still in place. Today the site is a remarkable preserved industrial heritage site within the national park, accessible by a long gravel road through the Wrangell Mountains.

name: Bannack, MT
description: Bannack was Montana's first territorial capital and a significant gold rush town in Beaverhead County, established after John White's gold discovery on Grasshopper Creek in 1862 set off a stampede of prospectors to this remote corner of what would become Montana Territory. The rough-and-tumble camp quickly developed a dark reputation — Henry Plummer, the town's elected sheriff, was in fact the leader of the 'Innocents,' a gang of road agents responsible for over 100 murders before a vigilante committee hanged him in January 1864. Bannack is now Montana's finest ghost town, preserved as Bannack State Park with over 60 original structures including the hotel, jail, gallows, and Masonic Hall still standing. Metal detecting is prohibited within the state park, but the site's remarkable preservation and rich history make it one of Montana's premier ghost town destinations.

name: South Pass City, WY
description: South Pass City was Wyoming's most significant gold mining ghost town, a booming camp in Fremont County on the South Pass of the Continental Divide in the Wind River Mountains, where gold discoveries in 1867-1869 briefly drew thousands of miners to this extreme and remote location and created one of Wyoming's first significant towns. The town had over 1,000 residents at its peak, with saloons, hotels, and a drug store, and was home to Wyoming's landmark women's suffrage movement — Esther Hobart Morris, who advocated for Wyoming's 1869 Women's Suffrage Act, lived here. The gold quickly played out and South Pass City declined to near-ghost town status within a few years, though it remained inhabited sporadically. Today South Pass City State Historic Site preserves over 30 original structures and interprets the gold rush and women's suffrage history.

name: Thurmond, WV
description: Thurmond was a thriving West Virginia coal town in Fayette County in the New River Gorge that at its peak in the early twentieth century was the largest depot on the Chesapeake and Ohio Railway's New River line, processing millions of tons of New River coalfield coal and generating more freight revenue than any other C&O station in the system. The town was notable for its non-stop gambling, prostitution, and saloons — Thurmond had a famously rowdy reputation even by coal town standards — and one poker game allegedly ran continuously for 14 years. The population declined as coal production shifted and rail operations modernized, and by the time New River Gorge National Park was established in 1978, Thurmond had fewer than a dozen residents. The historic C&O depot and a handful of structures survive as part of the national park's interpretive story.

name: Bulowville, FL
description: Bulowville was a small plantation-era and frontier community in Flagler County, Florida that existed along the Halifax River coastal region of northeast Florida in the antebellum period, connected to the Hernandez and Bulow sugar plantation operations that dominated the northeast Florida coastal economy in the 1820s-1830s. The community and surrounding plantations were destroyed during the Second Seminole War when Seminole warriors torched the plantations along Florida's east coast in 1836, forcing the abandonment of the entire coastal agricultural economy. The ruins of the Bulow Plantation sugar mill are preserved at Bulow Plantation Ruins Historic State Park, one of Florida's finest antebellum industrial ruins. The park and surrounding Flagler County coast is accessible, with the sugar mill ruins offering a tangible connection to Florida's violent plantation era.

name: New Salem, IL
description: New Salem was an 1830s Illinois frontier village in Menard County along the Sangamon River where Abraham Lincoln lived from 1831 to 1837, clerking in a store, serving as postmaster, studying law, and serving in the state legislature before relocating to Springfield to pursue his legal and political career. The village was reconstructed in the 1930s as Lincoln's New Salem State Historic Site, with 12 reconstructed log structures based on historical records and archaeology representing the town at its 1830s peak. The original New Salem declined quickly after Lincoln left — the Sangamon River proved not to be navigable as hoped, and the county seat went to nearby Petersburg — making it essentially a ghost town by 1840. The state historic site offers living history programs and archaeological interpretation of this important Lincoln heritage site.

name: Markham, FL
description: Markham was a small Glades County, Florida community in the Lake Okeechobee region of south-central Florida that developed as an agricultural support community in the early twentieth century as drainage projects converted the Everglades and Lake Okeechobee rim into sugarcane and cattle ranch country. The remote and flood-prone Glades County landscape made sustained community development extremely difficult, and small farming communities like Markham struggled to survive the periodic Lake Okeechobee hurricanes and floods that periodically devastated south Florida agriculture. The surrounding Glades County landscape today is active sugarcane and cattle ranching country with private land predominating.

name: Hiawatha, UT
description: Hiawatha was a Carbon County, Utah coal mining community in the Wasatch Plateau coal country, one of several company towns that served the extensive coalfields of central Utah that powered the state's railroad and industrial economy through the twentieth century. The town had company-owned housing, a store, and community facilities serving the miners and their families who extracted coal from the deep mountain seams. Hiawatha and neighboring coal camps declined as coal production consolidated and eventually rail and industrial coal demand fell, leaving the company towns economically stranded. The Carbon County coal country around Price, Utah is one of the West's most historically significant coal mining regions, and many former mine sites are accessible on public land.

name: Packard, KY
description: Packard was a small eastern Kentucky community in Pike County in the heart of the Appalachian coalfields, one of hundreds of coal camp communities that developed throughout the Cumberland Plateau from the 1880s through the mid-twentieth century to house the miners, their families, and the commercial operations that supported the coal industry. Pike County Kentucky is the most coal-rich county in America by historical production and was at the center of the union organizing battles and poverty that defined Appalachian coal country in the twentieth century. Former coal camp sites in Pike County are largely on private or corporate mining land, though the Appalachian cultural heritage preserved in these communities represents a rich chapter in American working-class history.

name: Mono Mills, CA
description: Mono Mills was a California lumber and sawmill community in Mono County at the edge of the Mono Basin, established in the 1870s by the narrow-gauge Bodie and Benton Railway to supply railroad ties and lumber to the booming silver mining town of Bodie in the White Mountains. The railway ran from the pine forest at Mono Mills to Bodie, carrying timber essential for the mines' underground timbering and surface construction at a time when Bodie had little local timber. When Bodie's mining boom faded in the early 1880s, the Mono Mills operation shut down and the railway was abandoned. The site of Mono Mills is now on BLM land east of Lee Vining, California, and portions of the old railroad grade and mill site remnants are visible in the landscape.

name: Callville, NV
description: Callville was an 1860s Mormon pioneer community on the Colorado River in what is now Clark County, Nevada, established in 1864 at the direction of Brigham Young as a river port to receive goods shipped up the Colorado from the Gulf of California, serving Utah's growing settlements without reliance on overland routes through hostile territory. The river port never achieved its commercial goals as the Colorado proved too difficult to navigate consistently, and when the transcontinental railroad was completed in 1869, overland shipping routes made the river port obsolete. The original site of Callville is now submerged beneath Lake Mead, flooded when Hoover Dam was completed in 1935. During significant Lake Mead drought-level drawdowns, a stone warehouse building from the original Callville has periodically emerged from the reservoir.

name: Johnsonville, TN
description: Johnsonville was a small Humphreys County, Tennessee community along the Tennessee River in western Tennessee that served as a Union supply depot during the Civil War, becoming the site of a significant Confederate cavalry raid in November 1864 when Nathan Bedford Forrest's artillery attacked and destroyed the supply depot, capturing or destroying an estimated $6.7 million in Union supplies and equipment. The raid — notable as one of the few instances of cavalry defeating a naval force — significantly impaired Union supply operations in the final months of the war. The site was flooded in 1944 when Kentucky Dam was completed by the TVA, submerging the original townsite beneath Kentucky Lake. Johnsonville State Historic Park on the lake's western shore preserves and interprets the battle site.

name: Dugger, TN
description: Dugger was a small Lawrence County, Tennessee coal mining community in the plateau region of northeastern Tennessee, connected to the small-scale coal operations that characterized the southern Appalachian coalfields in contrast to the massive coal industry of the central Appalachian region. The Tennessee coalfields were a smaller-scale operation than the Kentucky or West Virginia mines, and many small communities like Dugger developed and faded alongside individual mine operations. The surrounding Lawrence County landscape today is mixed farming and forest land in this hilly section of Middle Tennessee. Former Tennessee coal community sites are typically on private land in rural Lawrence County.

name: Ludlow, CO
description: Ludlow, Colorado was the site of the Ludlow Massacre of April 20, 1914 — the deadliest event in American labor history — when Colorado National Guard troops and company gunmen attacked a tent colony of striking coal miners and their families in Las Animas County at the southern end of the Ludlow anticline, killing at least 25 people including 11 children and two women who suffocated in a pit beneath a burning tent. The strike against the Colorado Fuel and Iron Company (owned by John D. Rockefeller Jr.) had begun in September 1913 in a dispute over wages, union recognition, and the notorious company town system that controlled virtually every aspect of miners' lives. The Ludlow Massacre galvanized the American labor movement and led directly to federal labor reform legislation. A monument maintained by the United Mine Workers marks the site, which is on the National Register of Historic Places and open to the public.

name: Midway, AZ
description: Midway was a small Yavapai County, Arizona community along the historic Black Canyon Road between Prescott and Phoenix, serving as a mid-point stage stop and watering hole in the central Arizona desert where travelers and livestock could rest before the next stretch of the demanding journey through the Sonoran Desert. The route through Bloody Basin and the Black Canyon corridor was one of Arizona Territory's most important north-south arteries before the railroad arrived, and communities like Midway served a vital if modest role in the frontier transportation network. The Black Canyon Highway (later Interstate 17) eventually made the old stage stops obsolete, and Midway faded into the desert landscape. The surrounding Prescott National Forest and private ranch land makes access to former stage stop sites in this region variable.

name: Swansea, AZ
description: Swansea was a small La Paz County, Arizona copper mining camp and smelter town along the Bill Williams River in the western Arizona desert, established in 1908 when the Clara Consolidated Gold and Copper Mining Company developed rich copper deposits in the Swansea Hills and built a smelter, housing, a school, and all the infrastructure of a functioning mining town. The smelter processed ore from several local mines through the 1910s before declining copper prices and ore depletion forced closure in 1924, and the buildings were subsequently stripped of usable materials. The ruins of Swansea — including the distinctive smelter stack and building foundations — are accessible via dirt roads on BLM land in the Swansea Wilderness area. The BLM-managed site is one of Arizona's more accessible copper mining ghost towns with visible structural ruins.
